meta.name: Tech Research Knowledge Base
meta.description: A curated, shareable library of foundational tech, AI, startup, design, and engineering media for builders.
meta.note: This file is the source of truth. Cards, hubs, indexes, and exports are generated from it via tools/generate.py. Edit data here, then regenerate.

hubs.title: AI Foundations
hubs.blurb: How AI actually works — the ideas, papers, and explainers that built modern machine learning and the transformer era.

hubs.title: AI in Practice
hubs.blurb: Applying AI as a builder: shipping ML systems, LLM products, and the engineering mindset behind them.

hubs.title: Startup History
hubs.blurb: How companies actually got started — first-hand accounts, hard lessons, and the canon of startup thinking.

hubs.title: Founder Mental Models
hubs.blurb: Durable ways of thinking about work, leverage, judgment, and doing great work over a career.

hubs.title: Product and Design
hubs.blurb: Designing things people love — product sense, interaction design, and the principles behind good craft.

hubs.title: Engineering and Systems
hubs.blurb: Systems thinking for builders: distributed systems, software design, and the papers that shaped the field.

hubs.title: Venture Capital and Fundraising
hubs.blurb: How venture works from both sides of the table — term sheets, incentives, and raising money well.

hubs.title: Internet and Big Tech History
hubs.blurb: Where computing and the internet came from — the people, labs, and machines that made it all possible.

hubs.title: Documentary and Long-Form Media
hubs.blurb: Films and long-form storytelling that capture the culture and history of technology.

hubs.title: Must-Watch Core Library
hubs.blurb: The essential cross-category set — if you only have time for a handful, start here. (Auto-collected from items marked 'core'.)

hubs.title: Conversation Starters
hubs.blurb: High-signal topics worth raising in technical and founder circles — each is something smart people genuinely argue about. A cross-cutting collection; every item also lives in its home hub.

learning_paths.title: Beginner Path
learning_paths.audience: Newcomers to tech, AI, and startups who want a clear, motivating on-ramp.
learning_paths.intro: Start with intuition and story before depth. These build a mental map of how AI works, where startups come from, and what good design feels like — without assuming a technical background.

learning_paths.title: Advanced Builder Path
learning_paths.audience: Engineers and technical builders who want depth in AI and systems.
learning_paths.intro: Primary sources and rigorous material. Expect papers, dense talks, and books that reward re-reading. Assumes comfort with code and basic math.

learning_paths.title: Founder / Startup Path
learning_paths.audience: People starting or operating a company who want judgment, not platitudes.
learning_paths.intro: The working founder's canon: company-building, fundraising, product, and the mental models that hold up under pressure.

learning_paths.title: AI Engineering Path
learning_paths.audience: Engineers and ML teams building with models, from fundamentals to frontier systems.
learning_paths.intro: A technical progression from machine-learning foundations through the architectures and scaling laws behind modern LLMs, ending with applied, build-it-yourself and state-of-the-field material.

learning_paths.title: Product & Design Path
learning_paths.audience: Designers, PMs, and product-minded founders building things people love.
learning_paths.intro: From the foundations of human-centered design and usability, through modern product discovery and process, to craft and prioritization in practice.

learning_paths.title: VC History Path
learning_paths.audience: Founders, operators, and investors who want to understand how venture capital actually came to be and how it works.
learning_paths.intro: From the broad history and mechanics of venture capital, through Silicon Valley's formation and its legendary firms, to fund economics and a critical counter-view. Moves from accessible narrative to deeper structural and empirical material.

items.title: Attention Is All You Need
items.creator: Ashish Vaswani et al. (Google)
items.summary: The paper that introduced the Transformer, an architecture built entirely on attention mechanisms with no recurrence or convolution. It became the foundation for virtually every modern large language model.
key_takeaways: Self-attention lets a model weigh relationships between all tokens in a sequence in parallel.
Removing recurrence made training dramatically more parallelizable and scalable.
The architecture generalized far beyond translation into the backbone of GPT, BERT, and modern LLMs.
items.why_it_matters: Almost everything in the current AI wave traces back to this architecture. Reading it is the single best way to understand what an LLM actually is.
items.audience_fit: Engineers, ML practitioners, technically curious founders.
items.conversation_note: It is the technical before-and-after line for the generative AI era.

items.title: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
items.creator: Alan Turing
items.summary: Turing's seminal essay asking 'Can machines think?' and proposing the Imitation Game (the Turing Test) as an operational substitute for the question. It anticipated and rebutted most objections to machine intelligence still debated today.
key_takeaways: Reframes an unanswerable philosophical question into a testable behavioral one.
Systematically pre-empts the major objections to machine intelligence.
Foreshadows machine learning by suggesting we build a 'child machine' that learns.
items.why_it_matters: The intellectual starting point of the entire field of AI, written before the first AI program existed.
items.audience_fit: Anyone interested in AI's foundations; non-technical readers welcome.

items.title: The Bitter Lesson
items.creator: Richard Sutton
items.summary: A short, influential essay arguing that across 70 years of AI research, general methods that leverage computation (search and learning) consistently beat approaches that build in human knowledge. The 'bitter' part is how often researchers resist this.
key_takeaways: General methods that scale with compute beat hand-crafted human knowledge over time.
Building in our own understanding feels good short-term but caps long-term progress.
Bet on learning and search, not on encoding domain expertise.
items.why_it_matters: A two-page mental model that explains the trajectory of modern AI and why scaling worked.
items.audience_fit: Founders, researchers, anyone making AI strategy bets.
items.conversation_note: It instantly opens the scale-versus-craft debate that dominates AI rooms.

items.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet)
items.creator: Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, Geoffrey Hinton
items.summary: The paper behind AlexNet, the deep convolutional network that crushed the ImageNet 2012 competition and kicked off the modern deep learning era by showing GPUs + big data + deep nets work at scale.
key_takeaways: Demonstrated that deep CNNs trained on GPUs dramatically outperform prior computer vision methods.
Popularized techniques like ReLU activations and dropout for regularization.
Marked the inflection point where deep learning became the dominant paradigm.
items.why_it_matters: The 'big bang' moment of the deep learning revolution — the result that made industry take neural nets seriously.
items.audience_fit: ML practitioners and engineers; historically minded builders.

items.title: Deep Learning (Nature review)
items.creator: Yann LeCun, Yoshua Bengio, Geoffrey Hinton
items.summary: An authoritative review of deep learning by three of its principal architects (later Turing Award winners), covering representation learning, backpropagation, CNNs, and RNNs in accessible form.
key_takeaways: Deep learning works by learning layered representations directly from data.
Backpropagation is the engine that makes deep nets trainable.
A single trusted overview of the field from the people who built it.
items.why_it_matters: The best single-paper orientation to deep learning, written for a general scientific audience.
items.audience_fit: Engineers and technically literate readers wanting one solid survey.

items.title: Neural Networks (video series)
items.creator: Grant Sanderson (3Blue1Brown)
items.summary: A beautifully animated video series that builds visual intuition for how neural networks learn, what gradient descent does, and how backpropagation works, all from first principles.
key_takeaways: Neural networks are layered functions that transform inputs into outputs.
Gradient descent and backpropagation are made visually intuitive, not just symbolic.
The best on-ramp for understanding the math without drowning in it.
items.why_it_matters: The single most effective way for a beginner to truly 'get' what a neural network is doing.
items.audience_fit: Beginners, designers, founders, anyone who learns visually.

items.title: Neural Networks: Zero to Hero
items.creator: Andrej Karpathy
items.summary: A free, code-first lecture series building neural networks and a GPT from scratch in Python, including the famous 'Let's build GPT' walkthrough. You write every line and understand every step.
key_takeaways: Build autograd, language models, and a working GPT from first principles in code.
Demystifies tokenization, attention, and training loops by implementing them.
The best practical bridge from 'I read the papers' to 'I can build it.'
items.why_it_matters: The definitive hands-on path to genuinely understanding modern LLMs by building one yourself.
items.audience_fit: Engineers and builders comfortable with Python who want depth.

items.title: Machine Learning (Stanford CS229 / Coursera)
items.creator: Andrew Ng
items.summary: The course that introduced millions of people to machine learning, covering supervised and unsupervised learning, regularization, and practical advice for applying ML. Famous for clear pedagogy over hype.
key_takeaways: Solid grounding in the classical ML toolkit before deep learning.
Emphasis on practical judgment: bias/variance, what to try next, error analysis.
Accessible to anyone with basic math and programming.
items.why_it_matters: The canonical first ML course; shaped how a generation of practitioners thinks about the field.
items.audience_fit: Beginners to intermediate; anyone wanting fundamentals.

items.title: Software 2.0
items.creator: Andrej Karpathy
items.summary: An essay reframing neural networks as a new kind of software: instead of writing explicit code, you curate datasets and let optimization write the program in weight-space. It reshapes how to think about building with ML.
key_takeaways: In 'Software 2.0', datasets and optimization replace hand-written logic.
The job shifts from writing code to shaping data and objectives.
Has profound implications for tooling, testing, and how teams are structured.
items.why_it_matters: A clarifying mental model for anyone building AI products — it changes what 'programming' means.
items.audience_fit: Engineers, founders, product leaders working with AI.

items.title: State of GPT
items.creator: Andrej Karpathy
items.summary: A talk (Microsoft Build 2023) explaining how GPT-class models are actually trained — pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, reward modeling, and RLHF — and how to use them effectively as a builder.
key_takeaways: Clear walkthrough of the full LLM training pipeline from pretraining to RLHF.
Practical guidance on prompting, tools, and where models fail.
Bridges research detail and applied product intuition in one sitting.
items.why_it_matters: One of the clearest explanations of how production LLMs are built and how to wield them.
items.audience_fit: Builders and product people shipping LLM features.

items.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
items.creator: Martin Kleppmann
items.summary: A rigorous, readable book on the fundamentals of storing and processing data at scale: replication, partitioning, consistency, consensus, batch and stream processing. The modern reference for backend and data engineers.
key_takeaways: Deeply explains the trade-offs behind databases, replication, and consistency models.
Connects academic distributed-systems theory to real engineering decisions.
Builds durable judgment about data systems rather than tool-specific trivia.
items.why_it_matters: The single most recommended systems book for backend engineers in the last decade.

items.title: The Mythical Man-Month
items.creator: Fred Brooks
items.summary: A classic collection of essays on software engineering and project management, most famous for Brooks's Law: adding manpower to a late software project makes it later. Still painfully relevant decades on.
key_takeaways: Communication overhead grows non-linearly as teams grow.
Conceptual integrity matters more than raw effort.
Many 'modern' management lessons were already understood in 1975.
items.why_it_matters: The original wisdom on why software projects slip — still cited because it's still true.
items.audience_fit: Engineering leaders, founders, anyone managing builders.

items.title: Concurrency Is Not Parallelism
items.creator: Rob Pike
items.summary: A crisp talk by a co-creator of Go drawing the distinction between concurrency (structuring a program as independently executing parts) and parallelism (executing them simultaneously). Clears up a near-universal confusion.
key_takeaways: Concurrency is about structure; parallelism is about execution.
Good concurrent design composes independent pieces that may or may not run in parallel.
Better mental models lead to simpler, more robust systems.
items.why_it_matters: A short talk that permanently sharpens how you reason about concurrent software.
items.audience_fit: Software engineers, especially systems and backend.

items.title: The Cathedral and the Bazaar
items.creator: Eric S. Raymond
items.summary: An influential essay contrasting two development models — the cathedral (closed, top-down) and the bazaar (open, decentralized) — using Linux as the case study. It helped legitimize open-source development to industry.
key_takeaways: 'Given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow.'
Open, iterative, community-driven development can outperform closed teams.
Release early, release often, and treat users as co-developers.
items.why_it_matters: Shaped the open-source movement and the way modern software is built and shipped.

items.title: Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events in a Distributed System
items.creator: Leslie Lamport
items.summary: A foundational distributed-systems paper introducing logical clocks and the 'happens-before' relation, giving a rigorous way to reason about ordering events without synchronized physical time.
key_takeaways: Introduces logical (Lamport) clocks and the happens-before relation.
Shows you can order distributed events without a shared physical clock.
Underpins much of modern distributed-systems theory.
items.why_it_matters: One of the most cited and important papers in computer science; the root of how we reason about distributed time.
items.audience_fit: Systems engineers and the theoretically inclined.

items.title: End-to-End Arguments in System Design
items.creator: J. Saltzer, D. Reed, D. Clark
items.summary: A classic paper articulating the end-to-end principle: functions are best implemented at the endpoints of a system rather than in its core. It shaped the design of the internet itself.
key_takeaways: Push application-specific functions to the endpoints, keep the network simple.
Explains why the internet's 'dumb core, smart edges' design works.
A reusable lens for deciding where complexity belongs in any system.
items.why_it_matters: One of the deepest design principles in computing, and a key reason the internet scaled.

items.title: Out of the Tar Pit
items.creator: Ben Moseley, Peter Marks
items.summary: A paper arguing that complexity is the single biggest cause of problems in software, distinguishing essential from accidental complexity and advocating for minimizing mutable state. A favorite of thoughtful engineers.
key_takeaways: Most software pain comes from accidental, not essential, complexity.
State and control flow are primary sources of accidental complexity.
Functional and declarative approaches can reduce the complexity burden.
items.why_it_matters: A rigorous articulation of why simplicity is the core engineering virtue.
items.audience_fit: Engineers who care about design and maintainability.

items.title: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (SICP)
items.creator: Harold Abelson, Gerald Jay Sussman, Julie Sussman
items.summary: A legendary MIT textbook that teaches programming as the art of controlling complexity through abstraction. Famous for being hard, deep, and permanently changing how you think about code.
key_takeaways: Programming is fundamentally about building and composing abstractions.
Covers higher-order functions, state, interpreters, and metalinguistic abstraction.
Rewires how you think more than it teaches any single language.
items.why_it_matters: Among the most influential CS texts ever written; a rite of passage for serious programmers.
items.audience_fit: Programmers wanting deep foundations; patient learners.

items.title: Founders at Work
items.creator: Jessica Livingston
items.summary: A collection of candid interviews with founders of major tech companies about their early, messy, uncertain days. It captures what startups actually feel like before they look inevitable.
key_takeaways: Successful startups looked fragile and improvised in their early days.
Persistence and adaptation matter more than a perfect initial idea.
Real founder stories, not sanitized retrospectives.
items.why_it_matters: A grounding antidote to startup mythology, from a Y Combinator co-founder.

items.title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
items.creator: Ben Horowitz
items.summary: A blunt, experience-driven book on the brutal parts of running a company that no one prepares you for: layoffs, near-death moments, demoting friends, and making decisions with no good options.
key_takeaways: There are no recipes for the hardest leadership decisions — only judgment under pressure.
Honest about the psychological toll of being a CEO.
Practical wisdom on management, hiring, and surviving crises.
items.why_it_matters: The definitive read on the operational and emotional reality of leading a startup.

items.title: Zero to One
items.creator: Peter Thiel with Blake Masters
items.summary: A contrarian set of essays on building valuable companies, arguing that great startups create something new ('zero to one') rather than copying ('one to n'), and that monopolies built on real differentiation drive progress.
key_takeaways: Build something genuinely new instead of competing in red oceans.
Durable companies have a real, defensible edge — a 'secret'.
Contrarian truth: ask what important truth few people agree with you on.
items.why_it_matters: A widely read framework for thinking about differentiation, monopoly, and ambition.

items.title: How to Start a Startup (Stanford CS183B)
items.creator: Sam Altman, Y Combinator, et al.
items.summary: A free Stanford lecture series organized by Y Combinator, with talks from Sam Altman, Paul Graham, Peter Thiel, Marc Andreessen, and others covering ideas, teams, product, growth, and fundraising.
key_takeaways: A structured curriculum covering the full arc of starting a company.
Direct advice from people who built and funded major startups.
Free and self-paced — effectively a startup mini-degree.
items.why_it_matters: The most comprehensive free startup curriculum, taught by the people who define the field.
items.audience_fit: First-time founders and anyone learning how startups work.

items.title: Do Things That Don't Scale
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: A Paul Graham essay arguing that early-stage founders should do manual, unscalable things — personally recruiting users, delivering delight by hand — because that's how startups actually get their first traction.
key_takeaways: Recruit your first users one by one; don't wait for growth to be automatic.
Deliver an overwhelmingly good experience to a few users early.
Startups don't take off by themselves — founders push them up the hill.
items.why_it_matters: Corrects the most common early-founder mistake: waiting to scale before earning traction.
items.conversation_note: Almost every founder can tell a 'we did the ugly thing early' story.

items.title: How to Do Great Work
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: A long essay distilling what people who do great work tend to have in common: working on things you're genuinely curious about, the discipline to follow that curiosity, and the courage to work on ambitious problems.
key_takeaways: Great work usually comes from deep curiosity, not forced ambition.
Choose problems at the intersection of what you're interested in and what's important.
Consistency, taste, and the willingness to look stupid compound over time.
items.why_it_matters: A modern, comprehensive guide to building an ambitious, meaningful career in any field.
items.audience_fit: Anyone serious about doing meaningful work — universal.

items.title: Maker's Schedule, Manager's Schedule
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: A short essay distinguishing the manager's schedule (chopped into hour-long meetings) from the maker's schedule (long uninterrupted blocks), and why meetings are so costly for people who build.
key_takeaways: Makers need long, uninterrupted blocks; a single meeting can wreck an afternoon.
Managers and makers operate on fundamentally different time models.
Protect deep-work time deliberately, especially in technical teams.
items.why_it_matters: A small idea that reshapes how builders and their managers should structure time.
items.audience_fit: Engineers, designers, and the people who manage them.

items.title: How to Get Rich (without getting lucky)
items.creator: Naval Ravikant
items.summary: An expanded podcast/essay series on building wealth through ownership and leverage — code, media, capital, and labor — plus the role of specific knowledge, accountability, and long-term games with long-term people.
key_takeaways: Seek wealth (assets that earn while you sleep), not money or status.
Modern leverage is code and media — permissionless and infinitely scalable.
Play long-term games with long-term people; build specific knowledge.
items.why_it_matters: A widely shared mental-model framework for leverage, ownership, and judgment.

items.title: You and Your Research
items.creator: Richard Hamming
items.summary: A famous Bell Labs talk on why some scientists do Nobel-caliber work and others don't. Hamming argues it's less about raw genius and more about courage, working on important problems, and managing yourself.
key_takeaways: Work on important problems — most people avoid them.
Open doors and ambient curiosity compound into great work over years.
Self-management, courage, and how you handle 'the system' matter enormously.
items.why_it_matters: A timeless meditation on how to aim a career at work that actually matters.

items.title: Poor Charlie's Almanack
items.creator: Charlie Munger (ed. Peter Kaufman)
items.summary: A collection of Charlie Munger's speeches and wisdom, centered on the idea of a 'latticework of mental models' drawn from many disciplines to make better decisions and avoid predictable folly.
key_takeaways: Build a latticework of mental models across disciplines, not siloed expertise.
Invert problems: study how to fail, then avoid it.
Understand human misjudgment and incentives to think more clearly.
items.why_it_matters: The foundational text for the 'mental models' approach to thinking and decision-making.

items.title: The Design of Everyday Things
items.creator: Don Norman
items.summary: The foundational text of user-centered design, introducing affordances, signifiers, mapping, and feedback. It teaches you to blame bad design, not users, and to see the designed world everywhere.
key_takeaways: Good design makes the right action obvious; bad design blames the user.
Affordances, signifiers, feedback, and mapping are core design vocabulary.
Usability is a property you can reason about, not an accident.
items.why_it_matters: The book that defined human-centered design and gave the field its shared language.
items.audience_fit: Designers, product people, engineers, founders — universal.

items.title: Inspired: How to Create Tech Products Customers Love
items.creator: Marty Cagan
items.summary: A modern guide to product management from Silicon Valley Product Group, covering how strong product teams discover and deliver products customers love — empowered teams, product discovery, and outcome over output.
key_takeaways: Empowered product teams solve problems, not just ship features.
Continuous product discovery de-risks what you build before you build it.
Optimize for outcomes (customer and business results), not output.
items.why_it_matters: The default reference for modern product management practice.

items.title: Inventing on Principle
items.creator: Bret Victor
items.summary: A landmark talk demonstrating live, immediate feedback between creators and their creations, and arguing that the most meaningful work comes from following a guiding principle. Visually stunning and intellectually provocative.
key_takeaways: Creators should have immediate connection to what they're making.
Tools shape thought — better feedback loops unlock better ideas.
A principle worth organizing your career around can be a north star.
items.why_it_matters: An endlessly influential talk on tools, creativity, and doing work that means something.

items.title: Ten Principles for Good Design
items.creator: Dieter Rams
items.summary: Dieter Rams's ten principles of good design (good design is innovative, useful, aesthetic, understandable, unobtrusive, honest, long-lasting, thorough, environmentally friendly, and as little design as possible). The bedrock of minimalist product design.
key_takeaways: 'Less, but better' — good design is as little design as possible.
Good design is honest, unobtrusive, and long-lasting.
A compact rubric you can hold any product up against.
items.why_it_matters: A concise design philosophy that influenced everything from Braun to Apple. (Exact origin year approximate.)
items.audience_fit: Designers, product builders, founders who care about craft.

items.title: How to Raise Money
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: A detailed Paul Graham essay on the mechanics and psychology of raising a seed round: getting to a strong position first, talking to investors in parallel, managing momentum, and not letting fundraising consume the company.
key_takeaways: Raise from a position of strength; be default-alive, not desperate.
Pursue investors in parallel to create real momentum.
Fundraising is a distraction — get it done and get back to building.
items.why_it_matters: One of the clearest practical guides to seed fundraising from someone who's seen thousands of rounds.
items.audience_fit: Founders raising or about to raise.

items.title: Secrets of Sand Hill Road
items.creator: Scott Kupor
items.summary: A clear insider explanation of how venture capital actually works — fund economics, term sheets, board dynamics, and what VCs are optimizing for — written by a managing partner at Andreessen Horowitz.
key_takeaways: Understand VC fund economics and why they shape investor behavior.
Demystifies term sheets, liquidation preferences, and governance.
Helps founders negotiate from understanding rather than fear.
items.why_it_matters: The most accessible explanation of the VC machine from the inside.

items.title: Venture Deals
items.creator: Brad Feld, Jason Mendelson
items.summary: A practical, founder-friendly guide to understanding and negotiating venture financings term by term, written by experienced VCs. The standard reference for what's in a term sheet and why.
key_takeaways: Walks through every major term sheet clause and its real implications.
Explains the negotiation dynamics and incentives on both sides.
Turns opaque legal documents into something founders can negotiate.
items.why_it_matters: The go-to handbook for founders who want to understand deals before signing them.
items.audience_fit: Founders, early employees with equity, operators.

items.title: The Mother of All Demos
items.creator: Douglas Engelbart
items.summary: Engelbart's 1968 live demonstration that introduced the mouse, hypertext, real-time collaborative editing, video conferencing, and windows — decades before they became mainstream. A staggering vision of interactive computing.
key_takeaways: Demonstrated the mouse, hypertext, and collaborative computing in 1968.
Framed computing as a tool to augment human intellect, not just calculate.
Set the agenda that Xerox PARC, Apple, and the web would later realize.
items.why_it_matters: The single most prophetic demo in computing history — the future, shown decades early.
items.audience_fit: Anyone interested in where modern computing came from.

items.title: As We May Think
items.creator: Vannevar Bush
items.summary: A 1945 Atlantic essay imagining the 'Memex', a device for storing and linking all of one's books, records, and communications by association — a striking anticipation of hypertext, personal computing, and the web.
key_takeaways: Imagined associative information linking decades before hypertext existed.
Anticipated the personal knowledge machine and the web.
Framed information overload and augmentation as central challenges.
items.why_it_matters: The conceptual seed of hypertext and the web, written before computers were programmable as we know them.
items.audience_fit: Anyone curious about the origins of the internet and information tools.

items.title: Hackers: Heroes of the Computer Revolution
items.creator: Steven Levy
items.summary: A history of the hacker culture from MIT's early labs through the home-computer and game eras, documenting the ethos — openness, hands-on imperative, mistrust of authority — that shaped tech.
key_takeaways: Defines the original 'hacker ethic' of openness and hands-on building.
Traces a cultural lineage from MIT labs to personal computing.
Explains values that still underpin open source and startup culture.
items.why_it_matters: The canonical chronicle of where hacker and builder culture came from.
items.audience_fit: Engineers and anyone interested in tech culture and history.

items.title: The Soul of a New Machine
items.creator: Tracy Kidder
items.summary: A Pulitzer-winning account of a team at Data General racing to build a new minicomputer, capturing the obsession, burnout, and craft of hardware engineering with novelistic detail.
key_takeaways: An intimate portrait of what building hard technology really demands.
Explores motivation, 'signing up', and the culture of intense engineering teams.
Timeless on the human side of ambitious technical projects.
items.why_it_matters: The classic narrative of engineering teams under pressure; still the best book on the feeling of building.
items.audience_fit: Engineers, eng leaders, anyone who's shipped under deadline.

items.title: Where Wizards Stay Up Late: The Origins of the Internet
items.creator: Katie Hafner, Matthew Lyon
items.summary: The story of the people who built ARPANET, the precursor to the internet — the researchers, the funding, and the engineering decisions (like packet switching) that made a global network possible.
key_takeaways: Tells how ARPANET and packet switching actually came to be.
Highlights the people and institutions behind the internet's origins.
Grounds today's internet in concrete, human engineering history.
items.why_it_matters: The definitive popular history of how the internet was born.
items.audience_fit: Anyone curious about the internet's real origins.

items.title: The Innovators
items.creator: Walter Isaacson
items.summary: A sweeping history of the digital revolution from Ada Lovelace to the web, emphasizing that innovation is collaborative — a relay of teams and ideas rather than lone geniuses.
key_takeaways: Digital innovation was driven by collaboration, not solitary genius.
Connects a long lineage from Babbage and Lovelace to modern computing.
A broad, accessible map of how the digital age was built.
items.why_it_matters: A single readable narrative tying together the whole arc of computing history.
items.audience_fit: Generalists and newcomers to tech history.

items.title: General Magic
items.creator: Sarah Kerruish, Matt Maude
items.summary: A documentary about General Magic, the 1990s Apple spin-off that tried to build the smartphone decades too early. It failed commercially but its alumni went on to shape the iPhone, Android, eBay, and more.
key_takeaways: Vision can be right while timing is fatally wrong.
'Failed' companies can seed an entire industry through their people.
A moving look at belief, ambition, and the cost of being early.
items.why_it_matters: A rare, honest film about a brilliant failure that quietly shaped modern tech.
items.audience_fit: Founders, product people, anyone who's bet on a big vision.

items.title: Something Ventured
items.creator: Dan Geller, Dayna Goldfine
items.summary: A documentary on the birth of the venture capital industry, following the early VCs who backed Apple, Intel, Genentech, Atari, and Cisco, and how their bets created Silicon Valley.
key_takeaways: Shows how a handful of early investors catalyzed entire industries.
Illuminates the founder–investor relationship at its best.
Grounds modern VC in its surprisingly recent origins.
items.why_it_matters: The clearest narrative of where venture capital came from and why it mattered.
items.audience_fit: Founders and anyone curious about VC's origins.

items.title: Triumph of the Nerds
items.creator: Robert X. Cringely
items.summary: A three-part documentary on the rise of the personal computer industry, from hobbyist garages to Apple, Microsoft, and IBM, told with first-hand interviews of the key players.
key_takeaways: Chronicles how the PC industry went from hobby to global force.
Captures the rivalries and accidents that decided who won.
First-hand interviews with the people who built the PC era.
items.why_it_matters: A lively, primary-source history of the personal computer revolution.
items.audience_fit: Anyone interested in PC and tech-industry history.

items.title: Stanford Commencement Address (2005)
items.creator: Steve Jobs
items.summary: Steve Jobs's widely watched commencement speech told through three stories — connecting the dots, love and loss, and death — distilling a philosophy of following curiosity and doing what you love.
key_takeaways: You can only connect the dots looking backward — trust they'll connect.
Do work you love; don't settle.
Remembering you'll die clarifies what actually matters.
items.why_it_matters: A short, durable piece of motivation for builders facing uncertainty.
items.audience_fit: Anyone — especially founders early in the journey.

items.title: Before the Startup
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: A founder-centric lecture on how to think before you start: how ideas emerge, how founders get misled, and how to choose problems.
items.why_it_matters: Paul Graham's influence on startup thinking is foundational, and this lecture shows the pre-company mental model YC pushes.
items.conversation_note: A natural entry point for anyone deciding whether to start a company.

items.title: Software Is Changing (Again)
items.creator: Andrej Karpathy
items.summary: A modern framing of Software 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0, arguing that natural language and LLMs are reshaping how software gets authored and operated.
items.why_it_matters: This is one of the best current conceptual maps for AI-native product teams.
items.conversation_note: It is one of the clearest ways to discuss AI-native software without falling into hype.

items.title: OpenAI DevDay: Opening Keynote
items.creator: Sam Altman and OpenAI
items.summary: OpenAI's first developer conference keynote, where major platform shifts for building with models were introduced.
items.why_it_matters: It marked a major transition from AI demos to AI platform strategy.

items.title: Tim Brown - From Design to Design Thinking
items.creator: Tim Brown
items.summary: A classic introduction to design thinking as a human-centered approach that links desirability, viability, and feasibility.
items.why_it_matters: It helped define the language of modern product/design collaboration.
items.venue: Transform 2009 / Mayo Clinic Center for Innovation

items.title: The three ways that good design makes you happy
items.creator: Don Norman
items.summary: A foundational talk on emotional design, showing why useful products still fail if they are not emotionally intelligible or pleasurable.
key_takeaways: Good design works at multiple levels
Pleasure and function reinforce each other
items.why_it_matters: It remains a concise bridge between psychology and product craft.

items.title: Marty Cagan on the Current 'Golden Era' for Product Management
items.creator: Marty Cagan
items.summary: A discussion about product management in the AI era, and why real product work is about solving problems, not administrating roadmaps.
key_takeaways: AI changes the tools, not the mission
items.why_it_matters: Marty Cagan remains one of the most influential voices for high-performance product teams.

items.title: Release It! • Michael Nygard & Trisha Gee • GOTO 2023
items.creator: Michael Nygard, Trisha Gee
items.summary: A production-hardened view of what fails in real systems and how engineers can design for resilience.
items.why_it_matters: A must-watch for teams that need to understand the difference between working code and survivable systems.

items.title: Steve Jobs Introduces the Macintosh
items.creator: Steve Jobs
items.summary: The famous Macintosh unveiling, a defining product launch that shows how narrative, reveal, and product demo can become culture.
items.why_it_matters: It is one of the most important product launch recordings ever made.

items.title: Steve Jobs: The Lost Interview
items.creator: Steve Jobs, Robert X. Cringely
items.summary: A candid long-form interview with Jobs between Apple eras, notable for his thinking on products, Xerox PARC, and Microsoft.
items.why_it_matters: It offers one of the clearest windows into Jobs' product mind outside of stage demos.
items.venue: PBS / Triumph of the Nerds archive

items.title: The Computer Chronicles - The Internet (1993)
items.creator: The Computer Chronicles
items.summary: A time-capsule view of the early internet before the web became mainstream.
items.why_it_matters: Excellent historical context for how quickly internet infrastructure moved from niche to mass market.

items.title: The Machine That Changed the World - Episode 1: Great Brains
items.creator: WGBH / BBC
items.summary: The first episode of the classic computer-history documentary series, tracing the earliest eras of computing and key pioneers.
items.why_it_matters: One of the best long-form introductions to the pre-personal-computer arc.

items.title: The Machine That Changed the World - Episode 2: Inventing the Future
items.creator: WGBH / BBC
items.summary: The sequel episode that moves the story into programming, commercialization, and the road toward personal computing.
items.why_it_matters: A strong companion to Episode 1 for seeing how the field became a mass industry.

items.title: How to Get Startup Ideas
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: A video reading of one of Paul Graham's most quoted startup essays: the best ideas are usually found, not invented.
items.why_it_matters: Useful as a compact reinforcement of YC's idea-selection philosophy, even if it is not the original lecture.
items.venue: Paul Graham / YC essay translated to video
items.conversation_note: Idea quality and problem selection are evergreen founder-room arguments.

items.title: Artificial Intelligence Index Report 2024
items.creator: Stanford HAI / AI Index Steering Committee
items.summary: A data-rich annual report tracking technical progress, investment, public perception, policy, and societal impact in AI.
items.why_it_matters: Best single macro reference for how the AI field is changing year to year.
items.conversation_note: It gives people a shared factual baseline instead of vibes.

items.title: CS224N: Natural Language Processing with Deep Learning
items.creator: Stanford University
items.summary: A thorough introduction to deep learning for NLP, including the fundamentals behind modern large language models.
items.why_it_matters: One of the cleanest public paths from NLP basics to the architecture and training ideas behind LLMs.
items.audience_fit: AI engineers, technical founders, ML product teams

items.title: CS231n: Deep Learning for Computer Vision
items.creator: Stanford University
items.summary: A deep dive into end-to-end deep learning for visual recognition, including CNNs, training/debugging, and modern vision architectures.
items.why_it_matters: A classic practical ML course that explains how deep vision systems are actually built and why they work.
items.audience_fit: AI engineers, applied researchers, product teams using vision

items.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
items.creator: He, Zhang, Ren, Sun
items.summary: Introduces residual learning to make very deep neural networks easier to optimize and more accurate.
items.why_it_matters: A foundational paper behind the practical success of deep neural nets across vision and beyond.

items.title: GPT-4 Technical Report
items.creator: OpenAI
items.summary: Describes GPT-4 as a large-scale multimodal model and summarizes its development, capabilities, and evaluation.
items.why_it_matters: A canonical public reference for what frontier model releases look like in practice.
items.audience_fit: AI builders, product teams, policy leads
items.conversation_note: It bridges model capability, product strategy, and the practical reality of frontier AI.

items.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
items.creator: Hoffmann et al. (DeepMind)
items.summary: Shows that many large language models are undertrained and derives compute-optimal scaling rules for model size and token count.
items.why_it_matters: Critical for understanding why modern foundation models are trained the way they are.
items.audience_fit: AI researchers, infra teams, applied ML leads

items.title: Computer History Museum Oral Histories
items.creator: Computer History Museum
items.summary: A large archive of firsthand interviews and demonstrations with pioneers in computing, entrepreneurship, and networking.
items.why_it_matters: One of the richest primary-source archives for tech history and industry memory.

items.title: Inside Apple Software Design
items.creator: a16z / Ken Kocienda
items.summary: An insider account of how Apple designed software in the Steve Jobs era, from Safari to the early iPhone and iOS.
items.why_it_matters: A rare primary-source look at software design inside one of the most influential product organizations ever.

items.title: The Playbook: Lessons from 200+ Company Stories
items.creator: Acquired
items.summary: A synthesis episode that distills lessons from hundreds of company histories into strategy, product, moats, and management.
items.why_it_matters: A compact distillation of the Acquired worldview, useful as an entry point to tech company history.

items.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design
items.creator: Jakob Nielsen / Nielsen Norman Group
items.summary: A canonical heuristic checklist for identifying and evaluating usability issues in interfaces.
items.why_it_matters: Still one of the most portable and field-tested design evaluation frameworks.

items.title: Continuous Discovery Habits
items.creator: Teresa Torres
items.summary: A system for weekly customer touchpoints, small research activities, and evidence-based product decisions.
items.why_it_matters: The clearest public framework for making product discovery continuous rather than occasional.

items.title: Shape Up
items.creator: 37signals / Basecamp
items.summary: A distinctive product development method built around shaping, betting, appetite, and fixed timeboxes.
items.why_it_matters: A useful counter-model for teams that are stuck in bloated roadmaps and endless backlog management.

items.title: Design Thinking: Human-Centered Innovation
items.creator: IDEO
items.summary: IDEO’s overview of design thinking as a human-centered method balancing desirability, viability, and feasibility.
items.why_it_matters: A useful strategic framing for design-led product and service work.

items.title: RICE: Simple prioritization for product managers
items.creator: Intercom
items.summary: Introduces the RICE prioritization model: reach, impact, confidence, and effort.
items.why_it_matters: A simple, durable prioritization framework for product roadmaps and startup decision-making.

items.title: Design Sprint Masterclass
items.creator: AJ&Smart
items.summary: A guided 4-day design sprint training program with facilitation resources.
items.why_it_matters: Useful when teams need to compress debate into a fast, testable decision process.

items.title: Refactoring
items.creator: Martin Fowler
items.summary: Defines refactoring as behavior-preserving improvement to the internal structure of code.
items.why_it_matters: Essential for any team maintaining code over time.

items.title: Working Effectively with Legacy Code
items.creator: Michael Feathers
items.summary: A practical guide to safely changing existing codebases without rewriting everything.
items.why_it_matters: One of the most useful books for teams inheriting messy or fragile systems.

items.title: Continuous Delivery
items.creator: Martin Fowler
items.summary: Defines continuous delivery as building software so it can be released to production at any time.
items.why_it_matters: A key mental model for product teams shipping frequently and safely.

items.title: The Pragmatic Programmer
items.creator: Andy Hunt, Dave Thomas
items.summary: A modern update to the software craft classic focused on practical, professional programming habits.
items.why_it_matters: A durable craftsmanship text for individual developers and small teams.

items.title: Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture
items.creator: Martin Fowler and collaborators
items.summary: A catalog of enterprise architecture patterns for layering, domain logic, persistence, and web applications.
items.why_it_matters: A strong language for discussing how larger software systems are composed.

items.title: Clean Code
items.creator: Robert C. Martin
items.summary: A guide to code readability, naming, structure, and professional programming habits.
items.why_it_matters: Still widely cited as a reference point for code hygiene and craft, even when teams disagree on specifics.

items.title: The Clean Coder
items.creator: Robert C. Martin
items.summary: A code-of-conduct style book about professionalism, estimation, testing, and responsibility.
items.why_it_matters: Useful for junior-to-mid engineers learning the social side of software craft.

items.title: The Lean Startup
items.creator: Eric Ries
items.summary: Introduces build-measure-learn, MVPs, and pivot/persevere thinking for startups and innovative organizations.
items.why_it_matters: The defining framework for modern startup experimentation and product iteration.

items.title: Essays by Paul Graham
items.creator: Paul Graham / Y Combinator
items.summary: A deep essay corpus on startup ideas, hiring, growth, fundraising, and founder psychology.
items.why_it_matters: Still one of the sharpest public collections of startup thinking ever published.

items.title: YC Startup Library
items.creator: Y Combinator
items.summary: A consolidated library of startup essays, videos, and talks from YC on how to start and grow companies.
items.why_it_matters: A high-signal living library from the most influential startup incubator in the world.
items.audience_fit: Founders, early operators, venture studio teams

items.title: The Mom Test
items.creator: Rob Fitzpatrick
items.summary: A compact guide to customer conversations that avoids flattering but useless feedback.
items.why_it_matters: One of the most practical tools for real customer learning before you build.

items.title: The Innovator's Dilemma
items.creator: Clayton M. Christensen
items.summary: Explains how successful companies can fail when new technologies start in low-end or overlooked markets.
items.why_it_matters: A foundational theory of innovation, strategy, and why new entrants beat incumbents.

items.title: Crossing the Chasm
items.creator: Geoffrey A. Moore
items.summary: Describes the gap between early adopters and the mainstream market for breakthrough technology products.
items.why_it_matters: Still essential for teams trying to move from niche enthusiasm to broad adoption.

items.title: Startup School
items.creator: Y Combinator
items.summary: YC’s online course for founders, taught by YC partners and successful founders.
items.why_it_matters: A practical founder curriculum that compresses years of startup trial-and-error into structured lessons.

items.title: Scaling Up Excellence
items.creator: Robert I. Sutton, Huggy Rao
items.summary: A framework for preserving quality while growing teams and organizations.
items.why_it_matters: Very useful once a startup begins facing growth-induced friction.

items.title: The Startup Way
items.creator: Eric Ries
items.summary: Extends Lean Startup principles to large organizations and continuous innovation systems.
items.why_it_matters: Useful for teams that need startup speed inside a larger company or studio.
items.audience_fit: Operators, innovation leaders, venture studio builders

items.title: How Will You Measure Your Life?
items.creator: Clayton M. Christensen, James Allworth, Karen Dillon
items.summary: A leadership and life framework that applies strategy thinking to personal and organizational choices.
items.why_it_matters: Useful as a grounding text for founders who are also thinking about character, meaning, and company culture.

items.title: What You Do Is Who You Are
items.creator: Ben Horowitz
items.summary: A book on company culture using history, examples, and practical advice for executives.
items.why_it_matters: Helpful for leaders designing the norms and habits of a venture studio or startup team.

items.title: The Cold Start Problem: How to Start and Scale Network Effects
items.creator: Andrew Chen
items.summary: A Talks at Google session with a16z general partner Andrew Chen, based on his 2021 book, on how products overcome the 'cold start problem' — the difficulty of getting a network-effects business off the ground when an empty network has little value — and how to grow network effects from a small working network to a tipping point and beyond.
key_takeaways: An empty network has little value; the 'cold start' is the hardest phase for network-effects products.
Start with a small 'atomic network' that works, then replicate it toward a tipping point.
Network effects must be actively engineered across stages: cold start, tipping point, escape velocity, and defending the moat.
items.why_it_matters: Network effects underlie many of the most valuable tech companies; this is a practical framework for the specific, underrated challenge of starting and scaling them.
items.audience_fit: Founders, growth and product teams, investors.

items.title: AI History (Stanford CS221: Artificial Intelligence)
items.creator: Stanford CS221 / Stanford Online
items.summary: A lecture segment from Stanford's CS221 (Artificial Intelligence: Principles and Techniques, Autumn 2021) tracing the history of AI — from early symbolic systems and expert systems through the rise of statistical learning and modern deep learning.
key_takeaways: Maps the swings between symbolic AI and learning-based approaches
Situates today's deep learning within decades of prior paradigms
Useful framing before diving into CS221's technical material
items.why_it_matters: Historical context from a rigorous university AI course helps builders understand why the field looks the way it does today.
items.audience_fit: Students, engineers, and builders new to AI's intellectual history.

items.title: AI & Machine Learning (Stanford CS106E Lecture Notes)
items.creator: Stanford CS106E
items.summary: Lecture notes from Stanford's CS106E (a computing course for general audiences) introducing artificial intelligence and machine learning concepts in plain, non-specialist language.
key_takeaways: Explains core AI/ML ideas without assuming a technical background
A gentle, readable on-ramp from a respected institution
Good primer before heavier courses or papers
items.why_it_matters: One of the most accessible institution-backed introductions to what AI and ML actually are.

items.title: Introduction to Graduate Artificial Intelligence (CMU 15-780)
items.creator: Carnegie Mellon University 15-780
items.summary: Introductory lecture slides from Carnegie Mellon's graduate AI course (15-780), framing the scope of AI: search, logic, probabilistic reasoning, and machine learning at a graduate level.
key_takeaways: Defines the breadth of academic AI beyond just deep learning
Graduate-level framing of search, logic, and reasoning
Primary course material from a top CS program
items.why_it_matters: A rigorous map of the AI field from one of the strongest CS departments, useful for grounding beyond the LLM-only view.
items.audience_fit: Engineers and researchers wanting graduate-level AI breadth.

items.title: AI Engineer (Conferences & Talk Library)
items.creator: AI Engineer
items.summary: The home of the AI Engineer conferences (Summit and World's Fair), which publish a large free library of technical talks for engineers building with LLMs and AI systems — covering agents, evals, RAG, tooling, and production practices.
key_takeaways: High-signal, practitioner-focused talks on building real AI systems
Strong coverage of agents, evaluation, RAG, and tooling
A living resource that tracks the fast-moving applied-AI frontier
items.why_it_matters: One of the best ongoing sources of applied, build-it-now knowledge for the emerging 'AI engineer' role.
items.audience_fit: AI engineers, technical founders, applied ML teams.

items.title: Building Faster with AI
items.creator: Andrew Ng
items.summary: A talk by Andrew Ng on how teams can build and ship faster using AI — emphasizing rapid prototyping with foundation models, agentic workflows, and treating AI as a speed multiplier for product development.
key_takeaways: AI tooling dramatically compresses prototyping and iteration time
Agentic workflows unlock new ways of building software
Speed of execution is itself a competitive advantage in the AI era
items.why_it_matters: Practical guidance from one of AI's most trusted educators on turning model capability into faster shipping.
items.audience_fit: Founders, engineers, and product teams building with AI.

items.title: Allan Dafoe — AI Governance & Safety Talks
items.creator: Allan Dafoe
items.summary: A collection of recorded talks by Allan Dafoe, Director of Frontier Safety and Governance at Google DeepMind, on the governance, policy, and safety challenges of navigating the transition to advanced AI.
key_takeaways: Frames AI governance as a central challenge of advanced AI
Connects technical capability trends to policy and safety
Primary-source thinking from a leading governance researcher
items.why_it_matters: As AI capability scales, governance and safety become strategic concerns for builders and policymakers alike — this is a credible primary source.
items.audience_fit: Founders, policy leads, researchers, AI strategists.

items.title: UI Talks — Product Management (Curated Collection)
items.creator: UI Patterns (UI Talks)
items.summary: A curated set of seven conference talks on product management (recorded 2013–2018) from speakers including Paul Adams, Jeff Gothelf, and Ken Norton, covering lean methods, rapid prototyping, and product strategy.
key_takeaways: Curated talks from respected product practitioners
Covers lean, prototyping, and product strategy
A compact playlist for leveling up product thinking
items.why_it_matters: A high-signal shortcut to several influential product-management talks in one place.

items.title: The Canon of Design for a New Journey in Product Management
items.creator: Rajiv Kaul (Intelligaia)
items.summary: A slide deck outlining a design-centered approach to product management, organized around trust, empathy, storytelling, prototyping, design systems, and user adoption as pillars of strong product experiences.
key_takeaways: Frames product management through a design-first lens
Connects design systems to product adoption
items.why_it_matters: A concise visual articulation of how design thinking and product management reinforce each other.

items.title: Artificial Intelligence (AI) by edX — Columbia University (Study Guide)
items.creator: Josh Wentz (notes on Columbia CSMM.101x, Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi)
items.summary: A detailed study guide documenting Columbia University's graduate-level edX AI course (taught by Prof. Ansaf Salleb-Aouissi), covering search algorithms, machine learning, neural networks, logic, NLP, robotics, and reinforcement learning across a 12-week curriculum.
key_takeaways: Summarizes a full graduate AI curriculum in one place
Covers classical AI (search, logic) alongside ML and neural nets
A roadmap/companion for self-studying the edX course
items.why_it_matters: A useful secondary-source map of a respected university AI course for self-directed learners.
items.audience_fit: Self-learners and engineers planning an AI study path.

items.title: Startup.com
items.creator: Chris Hegedus, Jehane Noujaim (dir.)
items.summary: A raw, cinema-vérité documentary following two childhood friends as they launch govWorks, a government-focused dot-com, during the internet bubble. It captures the dot-com boom and bust up close, including the strain it puts on the founders' partnership.
key_takeaways: An unfiltered, real-time view of a startup's rise and collapse
Shows how the dot-com bubble's hype and funding distorted decisions
A case study in how co-founder relationships fracture under pressure
items.why_it_matters: One of the most honest fly-on-the-wall records of startup failure and the dot-com era, valuable precisely because it isn't a sanitized retrospective.
items.audience_fit: Founders, co-founders, anyone studying startup risk and the dot-com era.

items.title: Silicon Cowboys
items.creator: Jason Cohen (dir.)
items.summary: A documentary on the rise of Compaq, founded in 1982 by three Texas friends — Rod Canion, Jim Harris, and Bill Murto — who built the first IBM-compatible portable PC and took on IBM in the personal computer market, with venture capitalist Ben Rosen backing them.
key_takeaways: An underdog challenger can beat an incumbent by out-executing on compatibility and speed
Compaq's IBM-compatible strategy helped open up the PC clone market
A vivid case study in 1980s startup ambition and hardware competition
items.why_it_matters: Tells a foundational and often-overlooked PC-industry story: how a startup challenged IBM and helped shape the open PC ecosystem.
items.audience_fit: Founders, hardware builders, anyone interested in PC and startup history.

items.title: VC: An American History
items.creator: Tom Nicholas / Harvard University Press
items.summary: The strongest single-volume history of venture capital in the United States, from early risk finance to modern Silicon Valley.
items.why_it_matters: It gives the broadest and most durable framework for understanding how VC became an American institution.

items.title: Creative Capital: Georges Doriot and the Birth of Venture Capital
items.creator: Spencer E. Ante / Harvard Business Press
items.summary: The classic account of Georges Doriot and ARD, often treated as the birth of modern venture capital.
items.why_it_matters: It explains how modern venture capital emerged as a distinct profession and structure.

items.title: The Money of Invention: How Venture Capital Creates New Wealth
items.creator: Josh Lerner and Paul Gompers / Harvard Business School Press
items.summary: A classic explanation of how VC works structurally and why it became a durable engine of new company formation.
items.why_it_matters: It remains one of the cleanest bridges between history and practice.

items.title: The Power Law: Venture Capital and the Making of the New Future
items.creator: Sebastian Mallaby / Penguin Press
items.subcategory: Silicon Valley and Modern VC Model
items.summary: A modern narrative history of the dominant firms, personalities, and strategy shifts that shaped the VC industry.
items.why_it_matters: Probably the best contemporary narrative of the modern VC industry.

items.title: How Venture Capital Works
items.creator: Harvard Business Review
items.summary: A concise evergreen explanation of the venture bargain: invest, nurture, and exit through sale or IPO.
items.why_it_matters: A clean structural primer for anyone trying to understand the industry.

items.title: How Do Venture Capitalists Make Decisions?
items.creator: Paul Gompers et al. / NBER
items.summary: A rare empirical look inside the black box of VC decision-making using a large survey of firms and partners.
items.why_it_matters: It reveals how real firms behave rather than how they advertise themselves.

items.title: Venture Capital Data: Opportunities and Challenges
items.creator: Steven N. Kaplan and Josh Lerner / Harvard Business School
items.summary: A foundational paper on why VC is hard to study and how data limitations shape what we know about fund performance and LP behavior.
items.why_it_matters: A necessary reality check for VC narratives built on incomplete public data.

items.title: A survey of venture capital research
items.creator: Marco Da Rin, Thomas Hellmann, Manju Puri / NBER
items.summary: A broad survey of the venture capital literature, including financing choices, fund behavior, and gaps in research.
items.why_it_matters: It helps you see what is known, what is contested, and what still needs study.

items.title: Financing New Ideas
items.creator: Harvard Business School / Georges F. Doriot exhibit
items.summary: A short exhibit on ARD and the early institutionalization of venture financing under Georges Doriot.
items.why_it_matters: A concise primary-source style entry point into the origin story.

items.title: Origins of High-Tech Venture Investing in America
items.creator: Tom Nicholas / Harvard Business School
items.summary: A historical account of how high-tech venture investing emerged in the United States and became tied to innovation growth.
items.why_it_matters: It explains VC as part of industrial and financial history, not just startup lore.

items.title: Institutions and venture capital
items.creator: Josh Lerner / Oxford Academic
items.summary: Explains how policy and institutions, including SBICs, helped shape the venture capital market.
items.why_it_matters: It shows VC was co-created by public and private institutions.

items.title: Silicon Valley
items.creator: PBS American Experience
items.subcategory: Silicon Valley and the Modern VC Model
items.summary: A high-quality documentary tracing the Fairchild-to-Intel-to-Silicon-Valley arc and the rise of startup culture.
items.why_it_matters: It situates venture capital within the broader history of Silicon Valley.

items.title: Bill Draper: The Rise of Sand Hill Road and Silicon Valley
items.creator: Stanford
items.subcategory: Silicon Valley and the Modern VC Model
items.summary: A pioneer VC reflects on the rise of Sand Hill Road and the evolution of the Valley from the inside.
items.why_it_matters: One of the clearest direct testimonies from the first generation of West Coast venture capitalists.

items.title: Interview with Don Valentine, 2004 April 21
items.creator: Stanford Silicon Genesis
items.summary: A major firsthand account from the Sequoia founder on the formation of Valley venture practice and company-building judgment.
items.why_it_matters: A rare window into the mind of one of the most influential venture investors ever.

items.title: Sequoia Capital (Part 1)
items.creator: Acquired
items.summary: A deep narrative on Sequoia's origins, strategy, and cultural influence.
items.why_it_matters: One of the best long-form treatments of a defining VC firm.

items.title: Cisco oral history panel: John Morgridge and Don Valentine
items.creator: Computer History Museum
items.summary: A firsthand record of how investor and operator dynamics helped build Cisco into a category-defining company.
items.why_it_matters: It shows how VC and startup leadership interact in real company formation.

items.title: The Economics within the Private Equity Partnership
items.creator: Victoria Ivashina, Josh Lerner et al. / Harvard Business School
items.summary: Explains how compensation, incentive design, and firm age shape GP economics and fundraising dynamics.
items.why_it_matters: Fundraising makes more sense once you understand the economic structure of the partnership.

items.title: Roundtable Summary: Towards a Better Aligned Private Equity World: Perspectives of Limited and General Partners
items.creator: Harvard Business School / ILPA
items.summary: A practical discussion of LP-GP alignment, transparency, co-investments, separate accounts, and fee pressure.
items.why_it_matters: It captures the modern LP concerns that now shape the fundraising market.

items.title: Chris Douvos: Limited Partners and Funds of Funds
items.creator: Stanford / Entrepreneurship Through the Lens of Venture Capital
items.summary: A clear LP-side explanation of fund-of-funds logic, portfolio construction, and what institutions seek from VC managers.
items.why_it_matters: A rare LP-focused view inside a GP-dominated conversation.

items.title: Venture Capital Booms and Start-Up Financing
items.creator: William Janeway, Ramana Nanda, Matthew Rhodes-Kropf / Harvard Business School
items.summary: A review of how VC booms change startup financing, the kinds of firms that get funded, and the downstream effects of capital surges.
items.why_it_matters: It explains why venture cycles do not just add capital; they change the market structure itself.

items.title: Oral History with Alan Patricof
items.creator: Computer History Museum / NVCA
items.summary: A first-person account of one of the earliest venture capitalists, with extensive reflections on angel investing and the evolution of the field.
items.why_it_matters: A direct witness account from a builder of the modern industry.

items.title: Oral History with Charles W. Newhall III
items.creator: Computer History Museum / NVCA
items.summary: A firsthand history of early venture, including Newhall's pre-VC exposure to angel investors and the Boston-to-California evolution of the field.
items.why_it_matters: Shows that modern VC grew out of a wider ecosystem of merchants, angels, and early risk capital.

items.title: Oral History with Henry F. McCance
items.creator: Computer History Museum / NVCA
items.summary: A detailed recollection of the early venture ecosystem, including the role of abundant angel and venture capital in the Valley.
items.why_it_matters: Useful for understanding how venture capital scaled from a niche craft into an industry.

items.title: From the Archives: Y Combinator Founders Paul Graham and Jessica Livingston on Studio 1.0
items.creator: Bloomberg Technology
items.summary: A widely cited interview on how YC began and how the accelerator model changed startup financing and founder support.
items.why_it_matters: One of the best primary interviews on the origin of the modern accelerator model.

items.title: Dalton Caldwell: Incubators, Accelerators, and Y Combinator
items.creator: Stanford
items.summary: A practical and historical talk on why accelerators emerged and how YC shaped the startup pipeline.
items.why_it_matters: It explains the accelerator model from someone inside YC's operating culture.

items.title: How Y Combinator Started - The Origin Story and Early Days
items.creator: Paul Graham / YC commentary
items.summary: A video treatment of Paul Graham's own account of how YC began and what problem it was trying to solve.
items.why_it_matters: A concise way to understand why YC became the template for accelerators.

items.title: Venture capital, angel financing, and crowdfunding of entrepreneurial ventures: a literature review
items.creator: Johannes Wallmeroth, Peter Wirtz, Alexander Peter Groh
items.summary: A broad research overview of angel financing, VC, and crowdfunding that maps the entrepreneurial finance landscape.
items.why_it_matters: A strong academic scaffold for understanding how angel capital fits into the broader startup financing stack.

items.title: The Determinants of Corporate Venture Capital Success
items.creator: Paul Gompers / NBER
items.summary: A landmark paper explaining the rise of corporate venture funds and the conditions under which they succeed.
items.why_it_matters: One of the clearest early academic histories of CVC as a distinct model.

items.title: Corporate Venture Capital as a Real Option in the Markets for Technology
items.creator: M. Ceccagnoli et al. / NBER
items.summary: Explains CVC as a strategic option for access to technology and a window on future markets.
items.why_it_matters: A more modern theoretical frame for why corporations back startups.

items.title: Have Secondaries Reached a Tipping Point?
items.creator: Bain & Company
items.summary: A high-quality industry report explaining the recent growth in secondaries and the liquidity pressure that helped drive it.
items.why_it_matters: A strong snapshot of how secondary markets moved from niche to central infrastructure.

items.title: Secondary Market: 2024 Recap & Outlook for 2025
items.creator: Coller Capital
items.summary: A detailed private-markets review showing how GP-led transactions and continuation vehicles are now a major part of the secondaries market.
items.why_it_matters: One of the clearest current sources for understanding the rise of continuation vehicles.

items.title: LP and GP led secondaries: Navigating different fund styles and continuation funds
items.creator: Industry video
items.summary: A practical explainer of LP-led and GP-led secondaries and the mechanics of continuation funds.
items.why_it_matters: Useful for understanding the structure behind the headlines.

items.title: The Entrepreneurial State
items.creator: Mariana Mazzucato
items.summary: A major critique arguing that public institutions often create the markets and technologies that private capital later monetizes.
items.why_it_matters: Essential for balancing pro-VC narratives with a serious account of public investment.

items.title: The Myths of Venture Capital
items.creator: INET Economics / Julia Ott
items.summary: A sharp discussion questioning the heroic mythology of VC and examining the industry's power and exclusions.
items.why_it_matters: A necessary corrective to celebratory VC history.

items.title: venture capital and the making of asset-driven inequalities
items.creator: Finance and Society / academic article
items.summary: An argument that VC can amplify asset-driven inequality through its selection, ownership, and exit patterns.
items.why_it_matters: Important for understanding the broader social consequences of the VC system.

items.title: The Next New Thing: Venture Capital Stories
items.creator: Computer History Museum
items.subcategory: Hidden Gems and Lesser-Known Historical Material
items.summary: A curated story collection of VC history featuring first-person recollections of major firms, founders, and deals.
items.why_it_matters: Excellent for building institutional memory without relying on promotional narratives.

items.title: Pioneers of Venture Capital
items.creator: Computer History Museum
items.subcategory: Hidden Gems and Lesser-Known Historical Material
items.summary: A classic history panel on how venture capital got started in Silicon Valley.
items.why_it_matters: One of the most important recorded conversations on VC's earliest years.

items.title: Venture Capital in the Valley: Past, Present & Future
items.creator: Computer History Museum / Something Ventured
items.subcategory: Hidden Gems and Lesser-Known Historical Material
items.summary: A recorded panel that places the early VC founding generation in conversation with later Valley developments.
items.why_it_matters: Useful connective tissue between the origin stories and the mature Sand Hill era.

items.title: How to Raise a Venture Capital Fund
items.creator: Winter Mead / Coolwater Capital
items.summary: A recent source on how VC funds are raised, how LPs think, and how emerging managers navigate institutional fundraising.
items.why_it_matters: Useful modern complement to the older LP/GP history literature.

items.title: Startup = Growth
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: One of Graham’s clearest statements that small differences in startup growth rate compound into radically different outcomes.
key_takeaways: A startup is a company designed to grow fast; growth rate is its defining metric.
Small differences in weekly growth compound into radically different outcomes.
Optimizing relentlessly for growth forces clarity about what users actually want.
items.why_it_matters: Gives a precise, widely-cited definition of what makes a startup a startup, anchoring how founders and investors reason about progress.
items.conversation_note: Founders and investors constantly argue about growth quality, and this essay gives them a shared language.

items.title: Founder Mode
items.creator: Paul Graham
items.summary: Argues that founders should sometimes manage companies differently from professional managers as they scale.
key_takeaways: Founders may need to run companies more hands-on than conventional 'manager mode' advice allows.
Blanket advice to fully delegate can hurt founder-led companies as they scale.
The idea is hotly debated precisely because the right limits of founder involvement are unclear.
items.why_it_matters: Sparked a major Silicon Valley debate about how founders should lead as companies scale, shaping current management discourse.
items.conversation_note: It touches a live Silicon Valley argument about founder autonomy, management, and culture.
